segments.s421-g17: پشین (an adverb/adjective) precedes the copula بود to mark antiquity or ‘former/ancient’. The clause follows with از + noun phrase (از تخمه کیقباد) where از introduces source/lineage: “from the seed/offspring of Kay Qobad.” Word order is: temporal/characterizing word + copula + prepositional origin.
segments.s421-g18: خردمند qualifies the person as ‘wise’. شاهی here describes his royal/kingship quality (a poetic adjectival/nominal use). دلش is دل + enclitic ش (his), and پر ز داد is the inverted poetic order of پر از داد (“full of justice”), with ز used instead of از; together: “his heart was full of justice.”
segments.s421-g19: همی رو چنین is an adverbial phrase: همی marks continuity/ongoing action (‘so/continually’), رو (here ‘manner/appearance’) + چنین ‘thus’ = ‘in this way’. تا introduces the temporal endpoint: تا فریدون شاه = ‘until Fereydun the king.’
segments.s421-g20: که is the relative pronoun ‘who/that’ referring back to فریدون شاه. شاه جهان بود is the main predication ‘was king of the world’ (شاه + complement جهان + past copula بود). و زیبای گاه is a coordinated nominal phrase: زیبای = ‘the beauty (of…)’ linked to گاه ‘the time/age’ (poetic construct, no izafe), giving ‘and the beauty of his age/time.’
segments.s421-g21: همان emphasises ‘that very’/‘the same’. مادرم is مادر + enclitic ـم ‘my’ (possession). The predicate is دختر قیصرست: دختر (daughter) + قیصرست (قیصر + copula ـست) = ‘is a daughter of Caesar.’ Word order places the demonstrative and possessor before the predicate.
segments.s421-g22: This is a relative/clarifying clause: کجا here has a rhetorical sense (‘where / moreover / in which case’), followed by the prepositional phrase بر سر رومیان = ‘over/upon the Romans’ (بر + سر used idiomatically to mean ‘over/above’ them). افسرست is افسر + ـست (copula) meaning ‘is an officer/commander’. Together: ‘who is commander over the Romans.’
segments.s421-g23: همان قیصر is the subject ‘that Caesar’. از introduces source: از + سلم = ‘from Salm’ (here Salm is a proper name). دارد نژاد is the verb phrase ‘has/possesses lineage’ (دارد = ‘has’, نژاد = ‘lineage’), so the clause means ‘that Caesar has lineage from Salm.’
segments.s421-g24: ز تخم فریدون = ‘from the seed/stock of Fereydun’ (ز is the poetic variant of از). با فر و داد lists accompanying qualities: با = ‘with’, فر = the characteristic Zoroastrian/epic ‘royal glory’, و = ‘and’, داد = ‘justice’. This gives the sense ‘from Fereydun’s stock, with glory and justice.’
segments.s421-g81: به is a preposition here meaning 'by/with' (introducing the manner/instrument). تنها (prepositive adjective) modifies تن, giving 'sole/lone body', and خوی + ش is the possessive formation 'own/his own' (خوی 'kinship/own' + enclitic ش 'his/her/its'). جستم is a past 1st-person form of جستن used transitively here ('I sought'); its direct object is نبرد ('battle'). Word order is preposition + modifier + noun + possessive clitic, then verb + object in the classical poetic style.
segments.s421-g82: به here again introduces the manner/circumstance ('with/by'), governing پرخاش 'reproach' as a kind of instrument or cause; تیمار من is a genitive phrase 'my care/concern'. کس نخورد is a compact clause: کس 'no one' + نخورد (past of خوردن) used idiomatically to mean 'took/received/paid heed' — literally 'no one ate/received it', i.e. 'no one attended to it'. The verb is simple past; the construction is typical of classical Persian idiom.
segments.s421-g83: سخنها (plural subject 'words/talk') is followed by the prepositional phrase به ما بر ('upon/to us'), then کنون 'now' and the past verb شد 'became' with predicate دراز 'long'. Literally 'the words to us have now become long' — i.e. 'our talk has grown long'. The verb شد functions as a copular/become verb linking subject to the adjective دراز.
segments.s421-g84: اگر is the conditional 'if'. تشنه‌ای is 2nd-person singular present/stative ('you are thirsty') with the enclitic -ی. جام is the object 'cup'. می‌را here represents the sequence 'می' (wine) + را (object marker); the dataset's lemma/senses do not supply the usual lexeme for 'wine+ra', so the appropriate sense is not available in the provided list. فراز functions adverbially/locatively 'up/on high' and in context gives the sense 'raise the cup aloft' (the imperative sense is implied by context rather than by an explicit imperative verb).
segments.s421-g73: به مردی is an instrumental prepositional phrase ('by/with manliness') placed before the subject for emphasis. من is the emphatic subject 'I'. آن باره را is the definite direct object ('that affair/matter') marked by را. بستدم is a past‑tense, first‑person singular verb; the form -دم is the older/poetic past ending (modern بستم).
segments.s421-g74: بتان را همه ('all the idols') is the object (را marks definiteness/emphasis) with همه reinforcing 'all'. بر زمین = 'onto/on the ground' (بر = on/upon + زمین). The second بر before زدم functions as a verbal preverb/particle (poetic repetition) giving a directional/over/down sense before the verb زدم 'I struck/struck down'.
segments.s421-g75: برافراختم is a compound verbal form (بر + افراختن) in the first‑person past 'I raised/displayed' with a poetic past ending. آتش زردهشت is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the fire of Zoroaster' (no ezāfe particle is written, a common poetic omission).
segments.s421-g76: که introduces a relative clause ('which' / 'that'). با مجمر = 'with/by a brazier' (با = with, مجمر = brazier/censer). آورده بود is the compound past (past participle آورده + auxiliary بود) giving a pluperfect sense 'had brought'. از بهشت = 'from Paradise/Heaven'.
segments.s421-g77: به here marks cause/instrument ('by' the victory). پیروزی = 'victory' and دادگر postmodifies خدای, forming 'the just God' (دادگر functioning as adjective/epithet). یک before خدای emphasizes 'one God' or singularity in a formulaic/poetic way.
segments.s421-g78: به ایران = 'to/into Iran' (به + place). چنان 'thus/so' modifies آمدم 'I came'; آمدم is first‑person past 'I came/arrived'. باز 'again' and جای 'place' together mean 'back to (my) place' — the word order is poetic (adverbial elements before the verb).
segments.s421-g79: که introduces a result clause ('so that'). ما را uses را with the pronoun for topical/emphatic marking ('for us' / 'against us'). به هر جای = 'in/at every place' (poetic location phrase). دشمن نماند literally 'an enemy was left' but here context gives the negative/result sense 'no enemy remained'; نماند is a past stative ('was left/ remained').
segments.s421-g80: به بتخانه‌ها در combines two locative markers for rhythmic/poetic emphasis ('in/at the idol‑houses'); در is the straightforward 'in'. برهمن 'Brahmin' is the noun that follows, and نماند again is the past stative 'was left' — together meaning 'no Brahmin remained in the temples.'
segments.s421-g65: همانا is a sentential adverb meaning 'verily/indeed' placed before the clause for emphasis. ندیدست is an archaic past-form meaning 'did not see' (verb before subject, a common poetic order). گور here is the subject and, in epic diction, denotes the wild ass/onager. از پلنگ is a simple prepositional object 'of/from the panther' (i.e. 'see a panther').
segments.s421-g66: نه introduces the second negative member ('nor'). از + شست forms a prepositional phrase 'by/from the thumb' (شست = 'thumb' here). ملاح is used of a seafarer/helmsman, and کام is the noun 'satisfaction/desire' (here meaning satisfaction). نهنگ (whale) is the possessor/affected noun; the phrase says the whale's desire was not satisfied by the helmsman's thumb. Word order is compact and proverbial, with the instrument/agent phrase placed before the possessed noun.
segments.s421-g67: ز هنگام is a poetic prepositional phrase 'since the time of'. Proper names تور و فریدون follow (Tur and Fereydun). گرد here functions adverbially ('around/about' or 'throughout') and helps locate the time span; the whole phrase sets a temporal frame.
segments.s421-g68: کس اندر جهان is an existential/negative phrasing meaning 'no one in the world' (کس = 'no one' here). نام این دژ is the noun phrase 'the name of this fortress' (possessive relation expressed by adjacency). نبرد appears in the line-final position as the main verb; in classical usage it would function as 'did not bear/take' (past), but the provided senses list does not include that verbal sense, so the exact listed senses do not match the usage here.
segments.s421-g69: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' or 'one', introducing the noun. تیره modifies دژ ('a dark/ominous fortress'). بر سر کوه is the locative phrase 'upon the summit of the mountain' (بر + سر + کوه), and بود is the past copula 'was' anchoring the clause in past narration.
segments.s421-g70: که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' linking to the preceding fortress. از برتری دور reads 'distant from superiority' (i.e. removed from seats of power); then از انبوه بود repeats the 'from' + noun pattern to say 'was distant from the crowd' — duplicated از marks separation from two different things. بود closes the relative clause ('it was').
segments.s421-g71: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. رفتم is the first‑person past 'I went/entered'. همه بت‌پرستان is the noun phrase 'all the idolaters' (subject of the following predicate). بدند is an archaic 3rd‑plural copula/auxiliary 'they were' (here functioning to state their presence or condition).
segments.s421-g72: سراسیمه is an adverb/adjective 'frantic, alarmed' describing their state. برسان is used as 'bring/bring together' (deliver/bring) and pairs with مستان 'the drunken ones' (object 'the drunkards'); بدند again is the plural 'they were' or 'they did' used after the clause to complete the predicate. The sequence literally describes: stressed by panic, they brought the drunkards.
segments.s421-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The phrase از رستم اسفندیار places the giver/source (رستم) before the recipient name (اسفندیار) in a slightly inverted apposition: 'Esfandiyar heard this from Rostam.' شنید is a past simple verb 'heard' that completes the clause; the object این ('this') refers to what was heard.
segments.s421-g2: This is the main clause: بخندید is the past-tense verb 'he laughed.' The coordinating و links the following descriptive phrase شادان (joyful) to the verb, and دلش بردمید is an idiomatic/periphrastic expression: دل (heart) + ـش (his) plus بردمید (a poetic form meaning 'was carried off / swept away'), i.e. 'his heart was carried away' = 'he was overjoyed.'
segments.s421-g3: بدو گفت is the fixed sequence 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', followed by گفت 'said'). ازین is the fused prepositional phrase از + این meaning 'about/from these'; رنج و کردار تو lists 'your hardships and deeds' as the content/topic of what was said/learned.
segments.s421-g4: شنیدم is the first-person past 'I heard / I have heard' used here in the sense 'I have heard of/learned about.' همه ('all') modifies the noun phrase درد و تیمار تو 'your pain and care', so the whole means 'I have heard all your pain and solicitude.'
segments.s421-g5: کنون is an adverb 'now' placing the statement in the present moment; کارهایی که من کرده‌ام is a relative clause 'the deeds/things that I have done' with کرده‌ام as the present perfect/past participle construction 'I have done.'
segments.s421-g6: ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'from/of' and introduces the group named گردنکشان ('neck‑cutters', i.e. rebels). سر برآورده‌ام literally places the noun 'head' before the perfect verb 'برآورده‌ام' (I have brought up/raised), i.e. 'I have lifted my head (above them)'. Note: the supplied senses for برآورده do not include the prototypical 'raise/bring up' meaning, so the best matching sense from the provided list is not available.
segments.s421-g7: نخستین 'first' is an adverb/adjective setting sequence. کمر بستم is an idiom 'I girded my loins' (کمر = waist/girdle, بستم = I bound/tied). از بهر دین is a prepositional cluster 'for the sake of religion' (از بهر = 'for / because of', دین = 'religion').
segments.s421-g8: تهی کردم is 'I made (it) empty / I emptied (it),' where تهی means 'devoid' and کردم is the past of 'to do/make.' از introduces the group removed (بت‌پرستان 'idolaters'), and زمین ('earth') is the locus: 'I made the earth devoid of idolaters.'
segments.s421-g33: بزرگی (direct object, fronted for emphasis) + ز شاهان من shows the source/ locus of that greatness ('from/among my kings'); یافتی is the 2nd-person singular past (you found/obtained). Word order omits the subject (تو) and places the object phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s421-g34: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). در بندگی is a locative/abstract phrase 'in servitude/devotion', تیز functions adverbially ('quickly/keenly'), and بشتافتی is the 2nd-singular past 'you hastened/rushed' describing the action that led to the greatness.
segments.s421-g35: ترا is the accusative 'to you' written as ت + را (2nd‑person suffix + object marker). بازگویم is 1st‑singular present/future-like 'I will recount/tell', همه هرچ هست = 'all/whatever there is' with هست as the copula ('is').
segments.s421-g36: یکی‌گر is a conditional 'if any/if one', دروغست is a nominal clause 'it is a lie' (دروغ + -ست = 'is a lie'), بنمای is an imperative 'show/reveal', and دست ('hand') is literal but idiomatic here — 'show your hand' = 'prove it / show the trick'.
segments.s421-g37: که تا introduces a subordinate circumstantial clause ('so long as / until'). شاه گشتاسپ را is marked by را as the (definite) noun phrase 'King Gushtasp' (object/possessive relation here), and داد تخت literally 'gave the throne' (داد = past 'gave'). The clause sets the temporal/contextual frame for the following statement.
segments.s421-g38: میان بسته is an adjectival/participial phrase 'bound/fastened in the middle' (modifying the previously mentioned تخت implicitly); دارم is 1st‑singular present 'I hold/keep'; به here indicates means/agency ('by/with'), followed by مردی ('manliness, courage') و بخت ('and fortune'). The whole expresses how the speaker held/kept the throne.
segments.s421-g39: هرانکس که = 'whoever (that)'; رفت is simple past 'went'; از پی دین is a phrase 'from (the) pursuit of religion' meaning 'having abandoned the faith' (پی = 'in pursuit of' used here with از to indicate leaving); به چین = 'to China' (چین = the country).
segments.s421-g40: بکردند is past 'they did / they made / they acted (thus)'; زان پس = 'after that / thereafter'; برو is an imperative 'go!' (here part of an exclamatory cry), and آفرین is an interjection 'bravo!' The clause reports the reaction/response that followed.
segments.s421-g49: دلم تنگ شد is a compact clause: دل + possessive suffix -م = “my heart,” تنگ شد is past tense ‘became tight’ (tang shodan) meaning ‘I was distressed/longed/grew anxious.’ بانگشان is بانگ (cry/crying) with the 3rd‑person plural possessive -شان = ‘their cry.’ بر زدم is the past 1st‑person (I) of زدن with the prefix بر‑, here meaning ‘to utter/sound (a cry)’ — together: “I cried out at their shout.”
segments.s421-g50: تن = ‘body.’ از دست آهنگران is a circumstantial phrase literally ‘from the hands of the smiths’ (از = from/by, دست = hands/agency, آهنگران = blacksmiths). بستدم is a past first‑person form of بستن ‘to bind/gird/fasten’ (poetic/older spelling), here meaning ‘I girded/fastened’ (i.e. put on a belt/armor or otherwise bound the body).
segments.s421-g51: برافراختم is past 1st‑person of برافراختن ‘to raise/lift up’ (I raised). سر = ‘head’ is the direct object. ز جای = ‘from (its) place’; نشست is the simple past form ‘sat/settled’ used here in coordinated narrative with برافراختم to mean he rose/changed position (poetic narrative can use classical forms in sequence). Together: ‘I lifted my head and moved/rose from my place.’
segments.s421-g52: غل و بند = two words for shackles/fetters (غل and بند) joined by و ‘and.’ بر هم شکستم is the verbal phrase meaning ‘I broke apart/into pieces’ (بر هم = to pieces/together; شکستم = I broke). به دست is an instrumental phrase ‘by/with (my) hand.’ The whole clause: ‘I broke fetters and bonds to pieces with my hand.’
segments.s421-g53: گریزان is a participial/adjectival form meaning ‘fleeing’ or ‘on the run.’ شد here acts as the past auxiliary ‘became/was’ to produce the sense ‘fled/ran away.’ ارجاسپ is the proper name (subject). از پیش من = ‘from before me / in my presence.’ Together: ‘Arjasp fled from before me.’
segments.s421-g54: بران سان is a two‑word adverbial: بران (بر آن) = ‘on that / thereupon’ and سان = ‘such / like,’ giving ‘in that way/so.’ یکی = ‘one’ (here = one person/someone) and نامدار انجمن = ‘renowned/famous of the assembly’ (نامدار = famed, انجمن = gathering/assembly). Literal: ‘One renowned person of that sort was in the assembly.’
segments.s421-g55: به مردی here functions adverbially as ‘in a manly way / like a man’ (به + مردی). ببستم is a poetic/past form of بستن ‘I girded / I tied on’ (past 1st person), used here of girding a belt; کمر بر میان = ‘a belt/around the waist at the middle’ (کمر = waist/girdle, بر میان = at the middle). Overall: ‘I girded my loins/waist like a man.’
segments.s421-g56: همی is a classical continuative/adverbial particle (often before past tense) meaning ‘kept on / was continually.’ رفتم is past 1st person ‘I went.’ از پس = ‘after / in pursuit of.’ چو = ‘like/as.’ شیر ژیان is a compound image ‘lion (شیر) of life/spirit’ — i.e. a vigorous/ferocious (living) lion. Together: ‘I went on after (them) like a living/valiant lion.’
segments.s421-g25: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same' placed before the name; سلم is the proper name 'Salm' (here a proper noun); پور is the patronymic marker 'son (of)' attached after the personal name; فریدون is the father's name (Fereydun); گرد here is a short postpositive word/epithet attached to the name — in epic Persian such postpositives often function as an epithet or an older verbal/adverbial element. The whole segment lists the person by demonstrative + name + patronymic + epithet.
segments.s421-g26: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a clause describing Salm; از here means 'from / among' (indicating 'among the kings'); خسروان is the plural 'kings'; نام is used as 'name' in the sense of reputation/renown; شاهی is the abstract noun 'kingship' (i.e. royal status); ببرد is the third-person singular present/subjunctive of بردن used narratively as 'bears' or 'carried' — literally 'he carries/takes' (so: 'who carried/bore the renown of kingship among the kings').
segments.s421-g27: بگویم is a verb form 'I say / let me say' (present-subjunctive or jussive used in narration) placed before the subject for poetic emphasis; من is the explicit subject 'I' coming after the verb (verb–subject order); و links the clauses; کس here means 'no one' in the negative clause that follows; نگوید is the negative form 'will not say' (third-person singular, present-subjunctive/jussive used for general negation); که introduces the subordinate clause 'that it is not'; نیست is the copula 'is not'. Together: 'I say it and no one will say that it is not.'
segments.s421-g28: که here continues the explanatory clause 'for/that' and introduces the reason; بی‌راه means 'wrong/unwarranted way(s)' (i.e. false paths); بسیار modifies it as 'many' (so 'wrong ways are many'); و is 'and'; راه is 'way/path' (here 'the true/right way'); اندکیست is a fused form اندک + است 'is few / there is little' (third-person singular present). The clause states the contrast that false ways are numerous while the correct way is scarce.
segments.s421-g29: تو is the emphatic 2nd‑person singular 'you'; آنی is a demonstrative/predicative 'that one' or 'the very one' acting as predicate nominal; که introduces a relative clause 'who ...'; پیش is the preposition 'before/in front of' (here 'before' in the sense 'in the presence of' or 'in front of'); نیاکان is 'ancestors'; من is the possessive 'my' (placed after the noun in old/poetic order). The clause sets up 'you are the one who, before my ancestors, ...'.
segments.s421-g30: بزرگان is a plural noun 'the great ones' (an epithet of nobles/elders); بیدار is an adjective 'awake/alert' placed after the noun (classical Persian often places adjectives postpositively); و is 'and'; پاکان is 'the pure ones' (another plural epithet); من is the possessive 'my' attached after the series, i.e. 'my great, my awake and my pure ones' — the segment is an appositive describing the ancestors.
segments.s421-g31: پرستنده is a deverbal agent noun 'worshipper/devotee' used predicatively; بودی is the past tense 'you were' (second-person singular past of بودن); همی is an adverb meaning 'always/ever' (classical continuous/emphatic marker); با is the preposition 'with'; نیا here stands for 'ancestors' (poetic form). The segment says 'you were always a worshipper with the ancestors.'
segments.s421-g32: نجویم is the first‑person singular verb 'I seek' but here includes the negative prefix ن- (classical negation) so contextually 'I do not seek / I will not seek'; همی again is an adverbial particle ('indeed/still' used for emphasis); زین is a fused form 'ز + این' meaning 'from this' (classical written as زین); سخن is 'speech/words'; کیمیا is 'alchemy' or figuratively 'the secret/elixir'. The clause therefore means 'I will not seek from these words the alchemical secret.'
segments.s421-g41: ازان پس (a fixed phrase) means 'from that time on' or 'thereafter'; که introduces a subordinate clause ('when/that'). ما را uses the object/experiencer pronoun with the postposed object marker را. به + گفت treats گفت (a deverbal or noun-like 'word/saying') as the oblique/instrumental ('by/at the word of ...'). گرزم is گرز (mace) with the 1st-person suffix -م 'my mace' and attaches to گفت as its possessor.
segments.s421-g42: ببستم is a 1st-person past-form verb ('I closed/tied/fastened' — here 'I was the one who caused the binding/closure'). پدر دور کردم is a causative or resultative sequence: 'I made/kept father distant' (دور 'far/away' functioning as predicate/adverb), with کردم as the 1st-person past 'I did / I caused'. ز بزم is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the banquet' (ز = from, بزم = feast/assembly).
segments.s421-g43: به لهراسپ marks the indirect object/recipient ('to Lohrasp'). از بند من is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of/from my fetters/captivity' (بند = fetter/bond, من = my). بد رسید is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'badly arrived' → 'bad news reached (him)' or 'it reached him as ill tidings', where بد qualifies the manner/value of the thing that reached and رسید is the past 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s421-g44: This clause states the result: ناپدید (vanished) is predicated with شد (became/happened) forming 'became vanished' (i.e., 'vanished'). از before ترک indicates cause/agentual source ('because of / by the Turk'), and روی زمین is the locative phrase 'from the face/surface of the earth' (روی = face/surface, زمین = earth). Word order places the auxiliary/verb شد before the rest for poetic effect.
segments.s421-g45: بیاورد is a past tense verb 'he brought' (3rd-person past). In poetic word order the verb appears before its subject; جاماسپ is the proper-name subject and آهنگران is the (direct) object 'the blacksmiths/metalsmiths'.
segments.s421-g46: که introduces a relative/purpose clause 'who/that'. ما را is the object 'us' with the object marker را. گشاید is a present/subjunctive 3rd-person singular of گشودن/گشادن meaning 'may open/loosen/free' (here 'free us'). ز بند گران = 'from (ز) heavy/weighty (گران) bonds/fetters (بند)', with the adjective after the noun as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s421-g47: همان کار آهنگران is a nominal phrase 'that same work of the smiths' (همان = that/same; کار = work; آهنگران = smiths in a genitive relationship). دیر بود is a predicative phrase 'was long/took long' (دیر here = long (of time); بود = was). The copula بود places the assessment in the past.
segments.s421-g48: مرا دل is an idiomatic dativo-experiencer construction: the clitic/segment م + را together function as 'to me' (i.e., 'I had a heart / my heart'), so 'مرا دل' = 'my heart (felt)'. بر + آهنگ + شمشیر = 'upon/for the purpose of the sword' where آهنگ means 'intention/aim' (here: the making or purpose) and شمشیر is 'sword'. بود is the past copula 'was' — together meaning 'my heart was set on (the) making/aim of a sword.'
segments.s421-g57: شنیدی is the 2nd-person past (perfect/simple) 'you heard/have you heard'. که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/what'), and در هفتخوان پیش من is a locative/temporal phrase ('in the Seven-Ḵwān / Seven Labours before me' — i.e. that befell me in those trials). Word order is literary: main verb + که + locative phrase that will be linked to the subordinate verb that follows.
segments.s421-g58: چه آمد is a fixed interrogative phrase meaning 'what came/what happened'. آمد is simple past 'came/happened'. The agents are expressed after the verb with poetic prepositions ز and از ('from, by'), so the clause literally says 'what came from the lions and from Ahriman.'
segments.s421-g59: به چاره is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'for a remedy / in search of a solution'. به + رویین‌دژ + اندر + شدم = 'I went into the iron/impregnable fortress': اندر is an archaic locative 'in/into' and شدم is the 1st-person past (I became/went in) used here as the past action 'I entered'. The doubled به marks purpose (to) then direction (into).
segments.s421-g60: جهانی is the direct object 'the whole/world'. بران گونه is a classical, slightly compressed phrase meaning 'in that manner' (بران = 'on/thereon/thereof', گونه = 'manner, way'). بر هم زدم is the verb 'I overturned/upset/disturbed' (برهم‌زدن = to throw into disorder); the verb is past 1st singular. Poetic word order places manner between object and verb.
segments.s421-g61: بجستم is a poetic, archaic past-form expressing an action by the narrator ('I ...'); here it governs the object همه کین ایرانیان 'all the vengeance/hatred of the Iranians' (همه = 'all', کین = 'vengeance/hatred', ایرانیان = 'the Iranians'). Word order is verb–object for rhetorical effect; the precise nuance of بجستم is literary/archaic (see note).
segments.s421-g62: به خون بزرگان is an instrumental/locative phrase 'with the blood/of the great ones' (به = 'with/into', خون = 'blood', بزرگان = 'great ones/nobles'). ببستم میان is a compound where ببستم (an older form related to 'بستن' or a variant sense given in the lexicon) plus میان ('middle/space/between') yields 'I filled/closed the middle' — i.e. I saturated the space with their blood. The verb is past 1st singular and the construction is tersely poetic.
segments.s421-g63: به توران و چین is a locative phrase 'in/to Turan and China'. آنچ (classical form of آن‌چه) is the relative/indefinite 'that which/what', followed by the perfect compound کرده‌ام 'I have done'. The clause is a literary relative: 'in Turan and China what I have done'.
segments.s421-g64: همان رنج و سختی is 'the same suffering and hardship' (همان = 'the same', رنج = 'suffering', سختی = 'hardship'). که introduces a relative clause 'that I have borne': که من برده‌ام, where برده‌ام is the perfect participle/compound meaning 'I have borne/experienced' (classical usage of بردن for 'to endure'). This is a standard Persian relative clause with the verb at the end.
segments.s421-g9: This is a nominal clause 'کس از جنگجویان گیتی ندید' functioning as the main negative statement. 'کس' with the negative past verb 'ندید' yields 'no one'; 'از جنگجویان گیتی' is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of the warriors of the world' (preposition 'از' + plural noun). The verb 'ندید' is past tense negative (did not see) and agrees implicitly with an understood subject (here 'no one').
segments.s421-g10: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause after the main negative verb (i.e. 'did not see that ...'). 'از کشتگان' literally 'of/from the slain' marks which persons are meant. 'خاک شد' is a periphrastic past: 'شد' (become) with 'خاک' (earth/dust) → 'became earth / was buried in the earth'. 'ناپدید' is a predicative adjective 'vanished', placed after the verb 'شد' to complete the idea 'became vanished/was lost'.
segments.s421-g11: 'نژاد من' is a possessive noun phrase 'my lineage' (noun + enclitic pronoun after the noun). The phrase 'از تخم گشتاسپست' is a prepositional origin phrase 'from the seed/stock of Gushtasp'; note the poetic fused form 'گشتاسپست' (a name form used in epic contexts). Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: possessor marked after noun, and origin introduced by 'از'.
segments.s421-g12: Again a relative/continuation clause introduced by 'که' ('that/whereas'), here elliptically reporting the ancestry: 'گشتاسپ از تخم لهراسپست' = 'Gushtasp is/from the seed of Lohrasp.' The copular verb is omitted or fused in poetic register (the name form 'لهراسپست' mirrors the earlier fused form). This is a paratactic listing of descent.
segments.s421-g13: 'که' again introduces a clause. 'لهراسپ بد پور اورند شاه' is an appositive identification: 'Lohrasp, (the) bad/ill son (پور) of Orvand Shah.' Here 'بد' modifies 'پور' to mean 'ill/wicked son'; 'اورند شاه' is the name + title 'Orvand the king' (name followed by 'شاه'). The adjective-noun ordering is typical of older epic Persian usage.
segments.s421-g14: This clause explains the reason/condition: 'که او را بدی از مهان تاج و گاه' = literally 'for/that he (او را, marked by object particle را as the one affected) suffered harm/ill from the nobles concerning crown and seat.' 'بدی' is a noun 'harm/evil' and takes 'از مهان' to indicate the source (the nobles/high men). 'تاج و گاه' is a compact epic collocation 'crown and seat/throne' (تاج = crown; گاه here functions in the sense of the royal place/seat).
segments.s421-g15: 'هم اورند از گوهر کی‌پشین' is a short nominal clause: 'also Orvand (اورند) is/was from the gem/essence (گوهر) of the former Kay (کی‌پشین).' 'هم' = 'also'; 'از' marks origin; 'گوهر' is literally 'gem' or 'essence' and is used metaphorically for noble stock.
segments.s421-g16: This clause is compressed and somewhat ambiguous in the manuscript tradition. 'که' links it to the previous line. 'کردی' here appears in a past verbal form ('you did') though in epic lines such forms can be elliptical or addressive; an alternative reading is the ethnonym 'Kurd' (ambiguous). 'پدر' functions as 'father/ancestor' (recipient/actor), 'بر' = 'upon/over', 'پشین' again appears as a name/epithet 'Pishin' or 'former', and 'آفرین' is ambiguous between the noun 'praise/bravo' and the verb-stem 'to create/praise' (a present stem form used poetically). In short, the clause says roughly that someone (grammatically 'you' or a referenced actor) 'made/caused the father to [praise/create] over Pishin', but the exact syntactic actor and whether 'آفرین' is nominal or verbal is left elliptical by the poet.

sentences.s421-s9: He belonged to the ancient stock of Kay Qobad; a wise ruler, his heart was full of justice.
sentences.s421-s10: So it went on like this until Fereydun the king — who was king of the world and the beauty of his age.
sentences.s421-s11: That very mother of mine was a daughter of Caesar, who moreover was commander over the Romans.
sentences.s421-s12: That Caesar traces his line to Salm; from the stock of Fereydun, with glory and justice.
sentences.s421-s41: I sought battle with only my own body; no one heeded my reproach or concern.
sentences.s421-s42: Our talk has now become long; if you are thirsty, lift the cup of wine up (raise the cup).
sentences.s421-s37: By my manliness I settled that matter, and I struck all the idols down to the ground.
sentences.s421-s38: I raised on high the fire of Zoroaster, which he had brought from Paradise in a brazier.
sentences.s421-s39: By the victory of the just God, I came back to Iran thus to my place.
sentences.s421-s40: So that in every place no enemy remained against us, and no Brahmin remained in the idol‑houses.
sentences.s421-s33: Truly the onager has not seen a panther, nor has the whale's desire been satisfied by the helmsman's thumb.
sentences.s421-s34: Since the days of Tur and Fereydun, no one in the world has borne the name of this fortress.
sentences.s421-s35: A dark fortress stood on the mountain's summit, which was remote from the seats of power and far from the throng.
sentences.s421-s36: When I went in, all the idolaters were there; panic‑stricken, they brought (together) the drunken men.
sentences.s421-s1: When Esfandiyar heard this from Rostam, he laughed and, joyful, his heart was carried away.
sentences.s421-s2: He said to him, 'I have heard of these your hardships and deeds; I have heard all your pain and care.'
sentences.s421-s3: Now the deeds that I have done, I have raised my head above the rebels.
sentences.s421-s4: First I girded my loins for the sake of religion; I made the earth empty of idol‑worshippers.
sentences.s421-s17: You gained greatness among my kings when you hastened eagerly into servitude.
sentences.s421-s18: I will tell you everything there is; if any of it is a lie, show your hand.
sentences.s421-s19: So long as I kept King Gushtasp's throne fastened in the middle by manliness and fortune.
sentences.s421-s20: Whoever left the path of religion and went to China — they did that; afterward people cried, 'Go! Bravo!'
sentences.s421-s25: My heart tightened and I cried out at their shout; I girded my body from the hands of the smiths.
sentences.s421-s26: I raised my head and rose from my place; I broke the fetters and bonds asunder with my hand.
sentences.s421-s27: Arjasp fled from before me; in that manner there was one renowned man in the assembly.
sentences.s421-s28: I girded my waist like a man, and I went after, like a living lion.
sentences.s421-s13: That same Salm, son of Fereydun, who among the kings bore the renown of kingship.
sentences.s421-s14: I will say it, and no one will say that it is not, for wrong ways are many and the (right) way is few.
sentences.s421-s15: You are that one who, before my ancestors, ranked among my great, awake, and pure ones.
sentences.s421-s16: You were ever a worshipper with (my) ancestors; I will not seek from this speech the alchemical secret.
sentences.s421-s21: From that time on, when word of my mace reached (them), I drove my father away from the banquet.
sentences.s421-s22: Word of my captivity reached Lohrasp, and because of the Turk he vanished from the face of the earth.
sentences.s421-s23: Jamasp brought blacksmiths who would free us from our heavy chains.
sentences.s421-s24: The smiths' work took a long time; my heart, however, was bent on making a sword.
sentences.s421-s29: Have you heard what befell me in the Seven Trials at the hands of the lions and of Ahriman?
sentences.s421-s30: Seeking a remedy, I entered the iron fortress; in that manner I upturned the whole world.
sentences.s421-s31: I roused up all the Iranians' vengeance; with the blood of the great I filled the middle (i.e. I drenched the place in the nobles' blood).
sentences.s421-s32: In Turan and China I have done what I have done — the very suffering and hardship that I have borne.
sentences.s421-s5: No one of the world's warriors saw that any of the slain sank into the earth and vanished.
sentences.s421-s6: My line comes from the seed of Gushtasp, and Gushtasp in turn is from the seed of Lohrasp.
sentences.s421-s7: That Lohrasp was the ill (or wicked) son of Orvand the king, for the nobles did him ill regarding the crown and the throne.
sentences.s421-s8: Arvand, too, came from the gem/essence of the former Kay; (and) that (one) you made (or he was made) father over Pishin — praise/creation (ambiguous in the line).
